title: Quantifying X-ray damage - Nature Energy
text:
Access through your institution

Buy or subscribe

Advanced X-ray spectroscopy techniques are widely used to probe chemical evolution in batteries, offering insights into ion transport, interfacial reactions and degradation pathways under operating conditions. However, the incident radiation used in these measurements can itself alter sensitive battery materials, complicating the interpretation of electrochemical processes in operando studies where changes are already dynamic. This makes it difficult to distinguish intrinsic behaviour from beam-induced artefacts, particularly in solid-state battery systems with complex interfacial chemistries. Now, Lena Mathies and colleagues in Germany present a systematic framework for quantifying and mitigating radiation damage during X-ray absorption measurements of solid-state electrolytes for lithium–sulfur batteries.Using synchrotron-based near-edge X-ray absorption fine structure spectroscopy, the researchers monitor the evolution of different solid electrolytes under increasing levels of X-ray exposure. By linking spectral changes to the amount of radiation delivered, they establish quantitative dose–damage relationships and identify exposure limits below which measurements remain effectively nondestructive. The approach enables beam-induced chemical modifications to be separated from intrinsic material behaviour, while also allowing safe exposure limits that can be used across different experimental setups and beamlines. The findings highlight the importance of accounting for beam effects when interpreting X-ray spectroscopy data from electrochemical systems.
